D – 5084 of 2024: M/s. Dawood Steel Fabrication Factory versus Collector of Customs and others. C.P. No. D – 5085 of 2024: M/s. Ifran Ullah Steel Mills & Furnace versus Collector of Customs and others. C.P. No. D – 5086 of 2024: M/s. Iftikhar Steel Mills & Furnace versus Collector of Customs and others. C.P. No. D – 5087 of 2024: M/s. Naseeb Steel Furnace versus Collector of Customs and others. Petitioners: Through Mr. Mufariq Shah, Advocate in all the petitions. Date of hearing: 15-10-2025 Date of decision: 15-10-2025 O R D E R Adnan Iqbal Chaudhry J. – These petitions seek quashment of separate FIRs lodged against Petitioners in June and July of 2022 by different Collectorates of the Customs at Karachi, for acts that constitute offences punishable both under the Customs Act, 1969 as well as the Sales Tax Act, 1990. Apart from submissions that go to the merits of the FIRs, learned counsel submits that the FIRs are liable to be quashed in view of the case of Directorate of Intelligence & Investigation-FBR v. Taj International (Pvt.) Ltd. (2025 SCP 235), decided on 04.12.2024, where the Honorable Supreme Court of Pakistan interpreted erstwhile provisions of the Sales Tax Act to hold that an FIR cannot be lodged C.P. Nos. D – 5067 to 5087 of 2024 Page 3 for the offence of tax fraud until tax liability of the offender is adjudicated by the department. Per learned counsel, said pronouncement would operate retrospectively to hit FIRs lodged prior thereto. However, the submission does not take in account the fact that each FIR also alleges offences punishable under the Customs Act, 1969, which are not subject matter of said pronouncement. Nevertheless, and without prejudice to the submission of learned counsel, he is queried whether same ground has been taken by the Petitioners before the trial Court under the alternative remedy available in section 265-K Cr.P.C.

However, the submission does not take in account the fact that each FIR also alleges offences punishable under the Customs Act, 1969, which are not subject matter of said pronouncement. Nevertheless, and without prejudice to the submission of learned counsel, he is queried whether same ground has been taken by the Petitioners before the trial Court under the alternative remedy available in section 265-K Cr.P.C. He concedes that it has not. In such circumstances, we dismiss these petitions with the observation that the Petitioners may avail the alternate remedy available before the trial Court under section 265-K Cr.P.C. Office shall place a copy of this order in all the listed petitions. JUDGE JUDGE SHABAN*
